S02.meta: // SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.
blocks: · Identify vague language in rubrics and convert it into observable criteria with Claude's help
· Draft analytic and single-point rubrics with Claude, then audit them before use
· Generate common-failure exemplars that show students what to avoid
· Create student-facing checklists from complex rubrics
· Apply a six-question rubric audit before distributing any Claude-drafted assessment tool

blocks: Argument — Strong, clear argument
Evidence — Excellent use of evidence
Analysis — Demonstrates sophisticated analysis

data.title: THE HUMAN GATE.
blocks: Are criteria observable? A reviewer of the student work and a reader of the rubric should be able to agree on whether th without additional interpretation from the teacher.
· Are performance levels distinct? Could a student's work sit plausibly in two adjacent levels? If yes, the level descript
· Is the rubric aligned to the outcome? Does each criterion measure something you said students would learn? A rubric can
· Are exemplars labeled and reviewed? AI-generated exemplars must be identified as constructed. Students should know the e

Is student language clear? A rubric written for your own grading logic may not work as a student-facing tool. Test it give it to a student (or a colleague unfamiliar with the assignment) and ask whether they could use it to self-assess be

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES
blocks: Accepting Claude's quality words.
Using AI-generated exemplars without review.
Building rubrics before defining the outcome.
Letting the rubric expand beyond what you actually grade.
Failing to share the rubric before the assignment.
